title: Interpretations Are Not Interpretations — They're Rival Theories

source.original: What Is Quantum Mechanics Really Telling Us? — David Deutsch & Brian Greene | World Science Festival

body_html:
One of the most persistent misnomers in physics is the phrase interpretations of quantum mechanics. The word suggests these are just different glosses on the same underlying formalism — stylistic preferences that don't affect the science. But as David Deutsch argues, borrowing the conviction of his former supervisor Bryce DeWitt, these so-called interpretations are actually distinct theories with different ontologies. They disagree on what exists, what happens during measurement, and what the mathematics refers to in the physical world.

The reason the terminology persists is cultural. Generations of physicists have been trained under the shadow of Niels Bohr's Copenhagen framework, which effectively declared certain questions off-limits. Students learn to calculate expectation values and transition amplitudes without ever asking what physical reality those quantities describe. The result is what Deutsch's other mentor, Dennis Sciama, lamented: when it comes to quantum foundations, the standard of argument drops to zero.

This isn't merely a philosophical quibble. If you treat the different approaches — Copenhagen, GRW collapse, de Broglie–Bohm, Everett — as mere window dressing, you lose the ability to ask which one is correct. And correctness matters: these theories make different claims about the structure of reality, and in some cases (like GRW versus Everett in the presence of large-scale quantum computers) they will eventually make different empirical predictions. Calling them all "interpretations" papers over exactly the distinctions that physics should be resolving.

The deeper issue is about the purpose of physics itself. Deutsch is blunt: the point of physics is not to track down the sixth decimal place of a prediction. It is to explain the seen in terms of the unseen — to articulate what is actually happening in the world that produces the phenomena we observe. A framework that refuses to answer that question isn't an interpretation. It's an abdication.
